dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for overdose/poisoning at --Address Redacted-- in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
My grandson-he came home unresponsive after a trip to the store with his sister and her boyfriend. I think it might be an overdose.
Can you tell me your name and today's date?
Eyes are open, but pupils are extremely pinpoint and both are reactive, though tracking appears dysconjugate.
No verbal response to loud voice or shaking. He is unresponsive.
No motor movement. He does not respond to touch.
Airway is partially obstructed-the head tilt and chin lift have maintained it so far. Any signs of choking or aspiration during this episode?
No, he wasn't choking or coughing.
We'll continue assisted breathing via BVM until we can verify airway patency.
He had agonal respirations initially-around 3-4 per minute-but his breathing has improved after BVM assist to about 8 per minute now.
Improved even more-now at 12 breaths per minute. Pulse ox is 92%.
Strong, rapid pulse at 118 and regular. Blood pressure is 147 over 81. Skin is warm, diaphoretic, and cyanotic. No bleeding detected.
Pulse increased to 133. Blood pressure slightly dropped to 124 over 80. Skin is now beginning to return color in patches.
Airway is patent post head tilt and chin lift with no signs of obstruction.
Based on this and prior info, suspect opioid overdose. We'll continue oxygen and BVM assist.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 3-unresponsive to verbal or tactile stimuli.
Eyes are approximately 2 mm in size, equal, reactive, and sluggish to light. PERRL observed at this point. No JVD or injuries noted. No DCAP-BTLS findings in the head or neck.
Chest rise and fall is symmetrical with BVM assist. Equal bilateral breath sounds. Some mild wheezing noted on inspiration and expiration bilaterally.
Skin is dry with residual cyanosis in the extremities. Central color is returning. Capillary refill is less than two seconds.
Arms show no signs of deformity or angulation. Two superficial lacerations just below the left anterior axillary fold. These appear recently self-inflicted by the grandmother's account.
Lower extremities show no swelling or edema. Gait appears unremarkable. No signs of trauma or instability.
Does he have any pain? Let's check his pain level.
Pain is zero, but I feel itchy.
Does he have a past history of substance use or recent hospital admissions?
Yes, he's struggled with opioids and depression. He was recently admitted to Tucker's Pavilion at Chippenham Hospital.
Has he been taking any prescriptions or illicit substances recently?
None that I'm aware of. He later said he feels itchy, but no other symptoms.
No known allergies.
He's not taking any medications currently, at least none that I found on him.
He has mild asthma.
When was the last time he had anything to eat or drink?
I'm not sure-probably within 48 hours.
He was out with his sister and her boyfriend and came home unresponsive, gasping for air.
You mentioned it's your 24-year-old grandson, weighing about 68 kg. That's correct?
That's correct.
Would you rate your pain on a scale of 0 to 10, 10 being the worst pain you've had?
Pain is zero, but I feel itchy.
Pulse is 133-fast and strong with no signs of irregularity.
Respiratory rate is 8-improving with BVM support.
Blood pressure is 124 over 80-some decline from earlier but still stable.
SpO2 is 92-improving with high-flow oxygen.
EKG is sinus tachycardia-no new changes detected.
Glucose levels are not detectable-no sugar found via test strips.
Initiating BVM ventilation and 15 LPM oxygen per standard order.
Turning on ECG monitoring now.
Starting ETCO2 capnography to assess ventilation effectiveness per protocol.
IV started in left antecubital vein using 20G catheter per standing order.
Administering 0.1 mg of naloxone IV per standing order-observing for respiratory improvement.
Respiratory drive improved with this round-patients now using less accessory muscles.
Now 119-heart rate is decreasing slightly.
Respiratory rate is 5-effort appears weaker now, so we'll give another dose.
Blood pressure is 143 over 90 post naloxone.
SpO2 is now 99%-improving despite low-flow O2.
EKG continues to show sinus tachycardia.
ETCO2 remains hypercarbic at 55-60 mmHg with box waveform.
Administering second 0.1 mg dose of naloxone IV now-respiratory effort is declining.
Revised vitals: pulse 119, respiratory rate 5, BP 143/90, SpO2 99%-nearly stable for transport.
GCS improved to 13-awakens only when spoken to directly. GCS is E=3, V=4, M=6.
Lacerations on left forearm are superficial, non-bleeding, and suspicious of self-harm.
Chest moves regularly-no asymmetry, pain, or discoloration noted.
Arms remain intact-normal movement and strength bilaterally.
No swelling, redness, or deformity in the lower extremities.
Transporting to Chippenham Hospital for PTA.
Patient is secured in a semi-Fowler position with shoulder and lap belts.
Current status: overdose, partial response to naloxone, stable on low-flow O2-transporting now.
IV and monitoring are active for hospital transfer.
Patient is safely loaded and secured for transport.
SpO2 is good and patient's GCS is 13. Improving, but needs close monitoring.
Capnography shows regular waveform. No signs of trauma.
After arrival, patient became combative and removed IV and monitoring equipment in the ER.
Patient resisted caregivers but was returned to hospital's care within minutes.
Patient is completely in hospital care now.
Following the medical-overdose protocol recommended under Protocol 7-1.
Field impression is Opioid overdose with signs of improvement following naloxone administration.
Patient is placed in ED room 11 per hospital request.
All procedures and vital signs have been transferred to receiving hospital team.
Equipment including EKG monitor and O2 are ready for hospital use.
Patient in ED room 11. All documentation on file.
